title: Interview Transcript
study: Social Integration and Academic Divergence: An Analysis of International Student Experiences at HKUST(GZ)

background_and_first_impressions.question: Could you briefly introduce yourself — your nationality, program, year of study, and how long have you been at HKUST(GZ)?
background_and_first_impressions.answer: My name is Ramazan. I am from Kazakhstan, but I am ethnically Uzbek. My program is Advanced Materials. I am a first-year student, and I have been at HKUST(GZ) for one semester — basically half a year.

background_and_first_impressions.question: What was your first impression of student life at HKUST(GZ), especially socially?
background_and_first_impressions.answer: I had never been abroad before coming to HKUST(GZ), so this experience was something I was not fully prepared for. There were a lot of friendly people around, and I genuinely liked the social side of things when I first arrived.

social_integration_and_belonging.question: How easy or difficult was it for you to make friends when you first arrived? Why?
social_integration_and_belonging.answer: In terms of making Chinese friends, it was not really that hard — maybe because there were many people willing to talk. There is a perception that Chinese students tend to stay among themselves and are not eager to interact with international students, and I partially agree with that. But I do believe there are people who are genuinely willing to be friends. Overall, I would say it was not so difficult for me.

social_integration_and_belonging.question: Do you feel that you belong socially at HKUST(GZ)? Why or why not?
social_integration_and_belonging.answer: Not really. For example, the clubs on campus are mainly conducted entirely in Chinese. I do not think they are very welcoming for international students — they tend to cater to Chinese students almost exclusively.

social_integration_and_belonging.question: Who do you mostly spend time with on campus — students from your own background, Chinese students, or a mix? What shaped that pattern?
social_integration_and_belonging.answer: Most of the time I spend with students from my own background. It is somewhat difficult to build friendships with Chinese students — partly because of the perception that they are not very open to international students, and partly because of the language barrier.

social_integration_and_belonging.question: Have you ever felt left out, disconnected, or excluded in social situations on campus? Can you describe a specific example?
social_integration_and_belonging.answer: I have not been involved in a lot of campus activities or events. In some English-language events I did feel a bit disconnected, but I think that was more because of me personally rather than the situation itself.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: How would you describe your interactions with Chinese students at HKUST(GZ)?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: I do not really know how the social situation compares to other universities, but I believe you can make some friends even here. There are people willing to talk to you if you look for them.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: What makes it easier to connect with Chinese students, and what makes it harder?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: Not many students here are highly proficient in English — they mostly speak Chinese in class and in everyday settings. Those who do have strong English skills are much easier to connect with. That is probably the biggest differentiating factor.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: To what extent do language differences affect your social relationships here?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: I think language is the single most important factor shaping my social relationships here, as I have mentioned. It defines who I can realistically connect with on a day-to-day basis.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: To what extent do cultural differences affect your comfort, confidence, or communication with others?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: Chinese culture and my own are quite different, but honestly I feel I am okay with that. I do not think cultural differences cause me major discomfort — I think I have adapted reasonably well.

interaction_with_chinese_students.question: Do you think students at HKUST(GZ) mix naturally across backgrounds, or do they mostly stay in separate groups? Why?
interaction_with_chinese_students.answer: There is definitely a tendency for Chinese students to stay together and not actively seek interaction with international students. That said, I still believe there are individuals who are genuinely open to connecting with students from very different backgrounds.

academic_experience.question: Do you think your social experience at university has affected your academic life? How?
academic_experience.answer: I do not think it has had a strong direct effect, but perhaps if I had better social relationships — particularly with Chinese students — my academic performance might have been better.

academic_experience.question: Has your ability or inability to build friendships influenced your motivation, confidence, or study habits?
academic_experience.answer: Building friendships gives you access to connections, and I think having Chinese friends specifically would be academically valuable — they tend to be very strong academically. That kind of peer influence can be a real advantage.

academic_experience.question: How has your social experience affected your access to study groups, academic help, or useful information?
academic_experience.answer: Sometimes professors speak in Chinese during class, and it is genuinely difficult to catch what was said. You have to ask Chinese students to explain, and if you are not well connected, that becomes a real obstacle. There have been cases where my limited social connections have hindered my access to information.

academic_experience.question: In group projects or collaborative assignments, have social or cultural dynamics affected your experience? Can you give an example?
academic_experience.answer: Yes. When I am the only international student assigned to a group of Chinese students, they tend to speak Chinese among themselves throughout the project. Even if you ask them to switch to English, they sometimes continue in Chinese anyway. It is a difficult situation — I am often unsure what to do.

academic_experience.question: Have you ever felt academically disadvantaged because you were not well connected socially? Why or why not?
academic_experience.answer: Yes, I think so. Chinese students likely have group chats — on WeChat and similar platforms — where they share academic information and resources. As an international student, I am probably never included in those, so I miss out on that kind of informal knowledge sharing.

academic_experience.question: Do you think students with stronger social networks generally perform better academically here? What makes you say that?
academic_experience.answer: I think so. Chinese students are generally very strong academically, and having closer social ties with them could give you access to study strategies and useful resources that you would not otherwise know about.

academic_experience.question: Have you noticed any difference in your performance between UCUG and UFUG courses? What do you think explains that difference?
academic_experience.answer: For me — and I believe for most international students — UCUG courses are much easier than UFUG. Interestingly, I heard that it is the opposite for Chinese students. I think this is because Chinese students have spent most of their academic lives preparing for rigorous subject-specific courses, so UFUG feels more natural for them, while the common core format is less familiar.

academic_experience.question: Do factors like language, class participation, or social connections affect your grades in UCUG and UFUG courses differently? How?
academic_experience.answer: Both types of courses are mostly taught in English, so language is generally manageable in terms of instruction. In terms of social connections, I think Chinese students share a lot of academic information through group chats and peer networks, which gives them an advantage that international students do not typically have access to.

institutional_factors_and_recommendation.question: What do you think are the main barriers preventing stronger integration between international and Chinese students at HKUST(GZ)?
institutional_factors_and_recommendation.answer: The language barrier is the most obvious one. But even if we all spoke fluent English, I think there would still be social barriers — some students are simply not very open to connecting with people from different backgrounds. There needs to be more openness and intentionality on both sides.

institutional_factors_and_recommendation.question: Do you think the university does enough to help international students integrate socially and academically? Why or why not?
institutional_factors_and_recommendation.answer: The university is only two years old, so there is room to grow. But as it stands, the clubs are conducted entirely in Chinese, which makes it very difficult for international students to participate. That approach actively hinders the social lives of international students rather than helping them.

institutional_factors_and_recommendation.question: What changes would you recommend to improve social integration and academic support for international students?
institutional_factors_and_recommendation.answer: The most immediate thing would be to make clubs available in English, or at least bilingual. More broadly, the university should hold more events in English. It feels like almost half of all campus events are in Chinese, which is disappointing for international students who want to be part of campus life.

institutional_factors_and_recommendation.question: Is there anything about the international student experience at HKUST(GZ) that people usually overlook or misunderstand?
institutional_factors_and_recommendation.answer: I think some of the everyday things international students do may come across as strange or unexpected to Chinese students. On the academic side, one of my Chinese friends was genuinely surprised that I was struggling with UFUG courses. He could not understand why I would be worried about the exam. That kind of expectation gap — where Chinese students assume UFUG should be manageable for everyone — is something that often goes unnoticed, and it can make international students feel their difficulties are invisible.

end_note: Transcript cleaned and organised. Original audio recording retained for verification.
